one_line_takeaway: AutoPentester turns PentestGPT's semi-manual advisory loop into a mostly hands-off, tool-executing LLM agent pipeline, cutting human interactions from ~15 to ~1 per HTB machine while raising subtask completion 27% and VM vulnerability coverage to 98%, though it still fully solves only 2 of 10 HTB boxes and is weak on web/GUI targets.
key_finding: On HTB machines AutoPentester beats PentestGPT on nearly all metrics: 59.92% vs 47.18% average subtask completion (a 27.0% relative gain), 85.64% vs 70.28% service coverage, 9.46 vs 11.23 steps (18.7% fewer), 0.30 vs 2.10 loops (~85.7% fewer), 1.13 vs 15.36 human interactions, and 0.13 vs 4.46 incomplete commands (97.7% reduction). On the custom VMs it reaches 98.14% average vulnerability coverage vs 70.37% (the paper's headline 39.5% relative gain) with far fewer steps and near-zero human interaction. It is slower (34.87 vs 20.31 min avg) and slightly costlier ($13.47 vs $9.61 avg tokens) because of the extra RAG/Verifier/ACI stages and forced wait times on interactive CLIs. In the ablation on Lame, the reasoning-based Strategy Analyzer alone lifts completion from 33.33% (baseline B) to 41.67% (B*), and stacking all modules (B*+R+L+V) reaches 100% with the RAG+Repetition-Identifier pair being the most critical combination; across the study RAG improves subtask completion by ~20.0%, the Repetition Identifier cuts loops-per-step by ~90.5% (0.48->0.05), and the Results Verifier cuts the incomplete-command ratio by ~80.1% (0.43->0.13). In the user survey AutoPentester averaged 3.93/5, 19.8% above PentestGPT (~3.3), losing only on Q6 (strategy-adjustment flexibility).
core_contribution: AutoPentester is a fully-automated LLM agent-based framework for penetration testing and vulnerability/threat assessment. Given only a target IP, it iteratively runs reconnaissance, scanning, vulnerability assessment, and exploitation using real CLI security tools and then produces a report. Its own novelty over the prior state-of-the-art (PentestGPT, which it uses as the baseline, not as its own work) is a five-agent architecture plus two non-LLM modules that raise the level of automation and remove most human intervention: (i) a Strategy Analyzer that maintains a modified attack tree (Pentest Tree/PTT) storing per-step key findings as attributes and uses findings-oriented Chain-of-Thought to pick the next step; (ii) a RAG-based Generator that retrieves tool-usage knowledge to emit complete, directly-executable commands; (iii) an Agent-Computer Interface (ACI) that actually executes commands via Python subprocess/pexpect against static and interactive CLIs (e.g., Metasploit); (iv) a Results Verifier that detects and repairs failed/incomplete commands; and (v) a Repetition Identifier that uses embedding cosine similarity to break exploitation loops. A Summarizer chunks long tool outputs and a Report Generator emits a CSV report (CVE, CVSS, remediation, etc.).
threat_model: This is an offensive-security (dual-use) automation paper rather than a defense or an attack-on-ML paper. The operating context is an authorized penetration-testing / red-team engagement: AutoPentester plays the role of the attacker against consenting targets (Hack The Box training machines and self-built vulnerable VMs) to surface exploitable weaknesses before real adversaries do. There is no adversary attacking AutoPentester itself; the paper does not analyze misuse, guardrails, or how to prevent the tool from being turned against unauthorized targets. The user study was ethics-approved (Univ. of Sydney HREC 2024/HE001529) and the paper notes automated exploitation needs careful handling in production.
limitations: AutoPentester only fully solved 2 of 10 HTB machines (Lame, Bashed); the other 8 failed a subtask, with failures attributed to inability to identify the correct strategy (4), missing knowledge of the required exploit (2, e.g., a LaTeX equation-generator exploit needing web browsing), and inability to navigate a GUI (2). It struggles with web apps because it leans on curl and loses focus on large response texts. In fully-automated mode it cannot drive GUI tools (mitigated by an interactive mode). RAG can narrow the Generator's scope so it misses corner cases (e.g., a specific GitHub exploit repo), so knowledge-base freshness is critical. The user study has only 10 participants (authors acknowledge the small sample). It is slower and more expensive per run than the baseline, and the 0.15 repetition threshold is a hand-tuned magic number. Evaluation is confined to training-grade targets (HTB, Metasploitable II, self-built VMs), not real production systems.

datasets_used: 10 Hack The Box machines (6 easy, 4 medium): Lame, Bashed, Active, Sau, Pilgrimage, Topology, Authority, Jupiter, Ambassador, Jarvis (5 reused from the PentestGPT study: Sau, Pilgrimage, Topology, Authority, Jupiter)
4 custom OWASP-Top-10 vulnerable VMs (VM1-VM4), where VM4 = Metasploitable II
RAG knowledge base built from 2 books (Metasploit: The Penetration Tester's Guide; Penetration Testing: A Hands-on Introduction to Hacking) plus HackTricks articles
User survey: 10 cybersecurity professionals (7 pentesters, 3 security professionals, 5+ years experience) evaluating reports for Active, Bashed, VM1, VM4
quantitative_results: HTB average subtask completion: AutoPentester 59.92% vs PentestGPT 47.18% (27.0% relative gain)
HTB average incomplete commands: 0.13 vs 4.46 (97.7% reduction)
HTB average time: 34.87 min vs 20.31 min (AutoPentester ~71.9% slower); average cost $13.47 vs $9.61
Custom VM average vulnerability coverage: 98.14% vs 70.37% (39.5% relative gain); VM4/Metasploitable II 92.59% vs 48.15%
VM average steps 7.17 vs 11.67, loops 0.17 vs 3.25, human interaction 0.25 vs 18.17, incomplete commands 0.00 vs 6.25
LLM backbone selection (avg HTB subtask completion): GPT-4-turbo 58.51%, GPT-3.5-turbo 42.32%, Gemini-2.0-flash 28.69% (GPT-4-turbo chosen)
Ablation on HTB Lame: B 33.33% -> B* (reasoning Strategy Analyzer) 41.67% -> full B*+R+L+V 100.0% (4.33 steps, 0 loops, 0 incomplete cmds)
Module contributions: RAG +20.0% subtask completion; Repetition Identifier -90.5% loops-per-step (0.48->0.05); Results Verifier -80.1% incomplete-command ratio (0.43->0.13)
User survey: AutoPentester mean 3.93/5 vs PentestGPT ~3.3 (19.8% higher); PentestGPT higher only on Q6
Repetition Identifier cosine-similarity threshold 0.15; RAG uses 500-char chunks, text-embedding-ada-002, top-10 retrieval; Summarizer uses 6,000-char chunks with 500-char overlap
Failure analysis over 10 HTB machines: 4 wrong-strategy, 2 missing-exploit-knowledge, 2 GUI-navigation; only Lame and Bashed fully completed
baselines_compared: PentestGPT (USENIX Security 2024) - primary baseline, chosen for shared purpose and public code

Ablation internal baselines: B (Summarizer+Analyzer+Generator, PentestGPT-like) and B* (B with reasoning-based Strategy Analyzer), plus module add-ons R (RAG), L (Repetition Identifier), V (Results Verifier)
LLM backbones compared: Gemini-2.0-flash, GPT-3.5-turbo, GPT-4-turbo
AutoAttacker and PenHeal discussed as related work but not run (no public code)
